required.title: [For Mentor] The Whole-Brain Child
required.author: Daniel J. Siegel and Tina Payne Bryson

required.title: [For Mentor] No-Drama Discipline
required.author: Daniel J. Siegel and Tina Payne Bryson

required.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] Stories of people who made mistakes and recovered

suggested.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] The Most Magnificent Thing
suggested.author: Ashley Spires

suggested.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] After the Fall
suggested.author: Dan Santat

competence_markers.description: They face mistakes directly — admitting what happened without habitual lying, hiding, or deflecting blame
They initiate repair after mistakes — moving toward cleanup, apology, or correction without being told every time
They receive correction calmly on most occasions — without meltdown, shutdown, or sustained anger
They try again after failure — returning to tasks or activities that went wrong, with willingness if not enthusiasm
They can articulate (in age-appropriate language) the difference between doing something wrong and being wrong
They accept consequences without extended self-pity or performance of distress
They recover emotionally from mistakes within a reasonable timeframe — upset is normal, but upset that lasts hours over a minor incident suggests shame processing, not guilt processing
They show early capacity to forgive others — accepting apologies and not holding mistakes over peers or siblings indefinitely

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you made a mistake.
narration_prompts.detail: What happened? How did you feel? What did you do about it?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between doing something wrong and being wrong?
narration_prompts.detail: Can you give an example? (The mentor may need to prompt with specific scenarios for younger students.)

narration_prompts.prompt: What do you do when you break something or spill something?
Tell me about a time you tried something and it did not work.
narration_prompts.detail: Did you try again? What did you do differently the second time?

narration_prompts.prompt: How does it feel when someone corrects you?
narration_prompts.detail: What do you do with that feeling?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time someone said sorry to you.
narration_prompts.detail: What did you do? Did you forgive them? What does it mean to forgive someone?

cross_connections.description: The daily routine provides the structure within which failure happens. A child who has a stable routine recovers from individual failures faster because the structure is still there. IC1.4 provides the emotional processing layer that IC1.1 needs when the routine breaks down.

IC1.3 teaches truth-telling and owning mistakes. IC1.4 provides the emotional architecture that makes truth-telling possible — a child who believes that admitting a mistake makes them a bad person will not tell the truth about the mistake. These two sub-domains are inseparable in practice.

IC3 builds the nervous system tools for handling big emotions. IC1.4 builds the moral and cognitive framework for what to do with those emotions after a failure. IC3 says "here is how to calm down." IC1.4 says "here is what to do once you are calm."

Physical activity is where many children first learn that failure is survivable. You fall off the balance beam. You miss the catch. You lose the race. These physical failures are training ground for IC1.4's lessons — if the child learns to get back up physically, they are building the pattern for getting back up in every other domain.

Shame is a tool of manipulation. Individuals and groups use shame to control behavior — "if you did X, you must be a terrible person." A child who understands the guilt/shame distinction and can resist shame-based identity attacks has the early form of what IC1.7 develops fully.

content.why_this_matters:
You are going to make mistakes. Not maybe. Definitely. You will break something. You will say the wrong thing. You will forget what you were supposed to do. You will hurt someone's feelings without meaning to. You will do something you know you should not have done.

This is not a problem. This is being a person.

The problem is not the mistake. The problem is what happens inside you after the mistake. Some people, when they make a mistake, feel so bad about it that they stop trying. They hide. They lie about what happened. They decide they must be a bad person because they did a bad thing. And then, because they believe they are bad, they stop trying to be good.

That is what this packet prevents. It teaches you something most adults never learned: **a mistake is something you did. It is not something you are.** You can do a wrong thing and still be a good person. You can break something and fix it. You can fail and try again. The mistake does not own you. You own the mistake — and what you do about it next is what matters.

This is one of the most important things you will ever learn. A person who knows how to make a mistake, face it honestly, fix what they can, and keep going — that person can do almost anything. A person who falls apart after every failure, who hides from every mistake, who believes that one wrong thing makes them a wrong person — that person is stuck. They cannot grow, because growth requires failing at things you have not learned yet.

You are going to learn how to fail well. That sounds strange. But it is real, and it is necessary, and it starts now.
content.what_this_develops:
This packet does not teach a skill you practice in isolation. It develops **patterns of response** that are observed over time when things go wrong:

1. **Facing a mistake directly** — not hiding, not lying, not pretending it did not happen
2. **Staying calm after correction** — receiving a correction without falling apart, shutting down, or becoming angry
3. **Fixing what you can** — the instinct to repair rather than to flee
4. **Trying again** — returning to the task or activity after failure without extended self-pity
5. **Separating what you did from who you are** — beginning to understand that a bad action does not make a bad person
6. **Accepting consequences without drama** — understanding that consequences follow actions, and this is normal, not cruel
7. **Recovering from emotional distress after a mistake** — being upset is normal; staying upset as a way to avoid action is not
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Everybody Makes Mistakes — This Is Normal

Here is something that is true: every person you admire has made mistakes. Your parents have made mistakes. Your grandparents made mistakes. The people in the stories you read — the brave ones, the strong ones, the ones who built things and led people and did great work — they all made mistakes. Some of them made serious ones.

Making a mistake does not mean you are stupid. It does not mean you are bad. It does not mean you are broken. It means you are a human being who is learning, and learning always includes getting things wrong before you get them right.

Think about learning to walk. Every baby falls down. Not once — hundreds of times. Nobody looks at a baby who falls down and says, "Well, that baby is a failure." They say, "That baby is learning to walk." The falling down is part of the walking. You cannot learn to walk without falling.

The same is true for everything else. You cannot learn to read without getting words wrong. You cannot learn to build things without some of them falling apart. You cannot learn to be kind without sometimes saying something that hurts. You cannot learn to be responsible without sometimes forgetting your responsibility.

**The question is never "did you make a mistake?" The question is always "what did you do next?"**

A person who makes a mistake and then lies about it has made two mistakes. A person who makes a mistake and then hides has not fixed anything — the mistake is still there, and now there is hiding on top of it. A person who makes a mistake and then decides they are terrible has turned a small problem into a big one — because now they have to deal with the mistake AND with believing they are terrible.

But a person who makes a mistake and says, "I did that. I am sorry. How do I fix it?" — that person has turned the mistake into something useful. They learned from it. They showed courage by facing it. And they showed everyone around them that they can be trusted to deal with hard things honestly.

### Part 2: The Difference Between "I Did Something Wrong" and "I Am Wrong"

This is the most important idea in this entire packet. It sounds simple. It is not. Adults struggle with it. But you are going to learn it now, when you are young, so it can grow with you.

When you make a mistake, there are two different things your mind might say to you:

**Voice One:** "I did something wrong. I need to fix it."
**Voice Two:** "I am wrong. I am bad. Something is wrong with me."

Voice One is helpful. It tells you the truth — you did something wrong — and it points you toward what to do next. Fix it. Apologize. Try again. Voice One treats the mistake like a problem to solve.

Voice Two is not helpful. It takes the mistake and makes it about who you are, not what you did. And if you believe Voice Two — if you believe that you ARE bad, not that you DID something bad — then fixing the mistake seems pointless. Why fix anything if you are just a bad person who will mess up again?

Voice One is called **guilt**. It says: "I did a bad thing." Guilt is uncomfortable, but it is useful. It motivates you to make things right.

Voice Two is called **shame**. It says: "I am a bad thing." Shame is not useful. It motivates you to hide, to lie, to give up, or to pretend you do not care.

Here is how to tell the difference in your own life:

- If you spill your milk and think, "I need to clean that up" — that is guilt. It is helpful.
- If you spill your milk and think, "I am so stupid, I always mess everything up" — that is shame. It is not helpful, and it is not true.
- If you forget your chore and think, "I need to go do it now" — that is guilt.
- If you forget your chore and think, "I am the worst, nobody can count on me" — that is shame.
- If you hurt someone's feelings and think, "I need to apologize and be more careful" — that is guilt.
- If you hurt someone's feelings and think, "I am a mean person" — that is shame.

**The mistake is always the same.** What changes is the voice you listen to afterward. You are going to practice listening to Voice One — the voice that tells you what you did and what to do next — and turning down Voice Two — the voice that tries to turn a mistake into your whole identity.

Your mentor will help you with this. When you make a mistake, they will not say "you are bad." They will say "you did this, and here is what happens next." That is on purpose. They are modeling Voice One for you until you can do it yourself.

### Part 3: Fix What You Break

When something goes wrong because of something you did, the first question is always: **what can I fix?**

Not "how do I feel about it?" Not "whose fault is it really?" Not "will I get in trouble?" The first question is: what can I fix?

- You spilled water on the table. Get a towel. Wipe it up. Done.
- You broke your sister's toy. Tell her what happened. Say you are sorry. See if it can be fixed. If it cannot be fixed, ask your parents how you can make it right.
- You forgot to feed the dog. Feed the dog now. Do not wait until someone reminds you. The dog does not care about your feelings — the dog needs food.
- You said something unkind. Go to the person. Tell them you said something wrong. Say you are sorry. Mean it.

**The repair does not have to be perfect.** A five-year-old who spills something and wipes it up with a towel — even if the table is still damp — has done the important thing. They faced the problem and took action. The towel technique can improve. The instinct to repair is what matters.

Some things cannot be fully fixed. If you break a plate, you cannot unbreak it. If you say something cruel, the person heard it — you cannot take the words back. But you can always do the next right thing: acknowledge what happened, make whatever repair is possible, and change your behavior going forward. The inability to fix something perfectly is not a reason to fix nothing.

**Fixing things is a habit.** At first, your parent will guide you: "You spilled that. What do we do?" Over time, you begin to do it automatically. The spill happens and your hand is already reaching for the towel. The unkind word leaves your mouth and you are already turning back to say sorry. That automatic response — mistake, then repair — is what IC1.4 builds.

### Part 4: Try Again

Failure is not the end of the story. It is the middle.

You tried to build a tower and it fell down. What do you do? You look at why it fell. Maybe the base was too narrow. Maybe you stacked the heavy blocks on top instead of the bottom. You adjust. You try again.

You tried to read a hard word and got it wrong. What do you do? You look at it again. You sound it out. You ask for help if you need it. You try again.

You tried to be patient and you lost your temper. What do you do? You calm down. You think about what happened. You try again tomorrow.

**"Try again" does not mean "pretend it did not happen."** You do not try again by ignoring the failure. You try again by learning from it. The tower fell — so you change how you build. The word was wrong — so you pay closer attention. You lost your temper — so you notice what set you off and prepare for it next time.

**"Try again" also does not mean "try again right this second."** Sometimes you need a break. Sometimes you need to walk away, calm down, and come back later. That is fine. The important thing is that you DO come back. Walking away to calm down is healthy. Walking away forever because you are afraid of failing again is not.

The people who accomplish the most in life are not the people who never fail. They are the people who fail, learn, and try again more times than everyone else. Every master was once a beginner who refused to stop after the first failure.

### Part 5: What Calm Correction Looks Like

When you make a mistake, the adult in your life will correct you. This is their job. Correction is not punishment for being bad. Correction is information about what went wrong and what to do differently.

**What correction should feel like:**

- Clear: "You left your shoes in the hallway. Go put them where they belong."
- Calm: The adult is not yelling. They are not angry at who you are. They are pointing out what you did.
- Specific: They tell you what went wrong and what to do about it.
- Finished: After you make the correction, it is over. You do not need to keep feeling bad about it for the rest of the day.

**What correction is NOT:**

- It is not an attack on who you are: "What is wrong with you?" is not correction. It is shame.
- It is not endless: being reminded of the same mistake for hours or days is not correction. It is punishment that teaches nothing.
- It is not unpredictable: the same mistake should get the same response, whether the adult is in a good mood or a bad one.

**What YOU do when you are corrected:**

1. Listen. Do not argue, do not explain, do not make excuses.
2. Say "okay" or "I understand" or "I am sorry."
3. Do whatever needs to be done to fix it.
4. Move on. The correction is over. You do not need to carry it with you.

This is harder than it sounds, especially when you feel embarrassed or upset. Your face might get hot. Your eyes might water. You might feel angry. All of those feelings are normal. Let them happen. They will pass. What matters is what you do — and what you do is listen, fix it, and move on.

### Part 6: When Someone Else Makes a Mistake

This packet is not only about your mistakes. It is also about how you respond when someone else makes one.

When your brother breaks your toy, when your friend says something unkind, when another child does something wrong — you have a choice about how to respond.

**What the right response looks like:**

- If someone hurts you by accident, let them make it right. Do not hold onto it longer than necessary.
- If someone apologizes and means it, accept the apology. Forgiveness does not mean pretending it did not happen. It means allowing the person to move forward after they have tried to make repair.
- If someone is struggling because they made a mistake, do not pile on. They already know they messed up. You do not need to make sure they feel worse.

**What the wrong response looks like:**

- Refusing to let someone make repair — holding the mistake over them permanently
- Telling everyone else what the person did wrong — turning their mistake into a public event
- Using someone's mistake to feel superior — "At least I did not do THAT"

You will want other people to be kind to you when you fail. Practice being kind to them when they fail. This is not softness. It is the foundation of every good relationship you will ever have.

### Part 7: When You Feel Like Hiding

Sometimes after a mistake, you will feel a strong pull to hide. To pretend it did not happen. To lie. To blame someone else. To run to your room and refuse to talk about it.

That pull is shame. It is the feeling that if anyone sees what you did, they will see that you are bad. So you hide the evidence. You cover up. You deflect.

**Every time you hide instead of facing it, the shame gets stronger.** Because now you have a secret. And the secret tells you that whatever you did must be really bad — so bad that you had to hide it. And that makes you feel worse. And feeling worse makes you want to hide more. It is a circle that goes down and down.

**Every time you face it instead of hiding, the shame gets weaker.** Because you discover something: the thing you were so afraid of — being seen after a mistake — is survivable. Your parent did not stop loving you. Your friend did not leave. The world did not end. You made a mistake, you faced it, and life continued.

This is the most important practice in this entire packet: **when you feel the pull to hide, go toward the thing you want to hide from.** Tell the truth. Show what happened. Ask for help. It will be uncomfortable. It will also be over faster, and you will be stronger for having done it.

Your mentor knows this. They will create an environment where telling the truth about a mistake is safer than hiding from it. But ultimately, the courage to face failure is yours to build. Nobody can give it to you. You build it one honest moment at a time.
content.formative_practices:
These are not one-time activities. They are ongoing practices that form how the student responds to failure over time.

### Practice 1: The Repair Instinct [As Mistakes Happen]
When you make a mistake — spill something, break something, forget something, say the wrong thing — your first action is repair. Not explanation, not emotion, not avoidance. Repair. Clean it up, fix it, apologize, do the thing you forgot. The mentor guides this initially: "You spilled that. What do we do?" Over time, the student initiates repair without prompting.

### Practice 2: Voice One / Voice Two Check [After Mistakes, With Mentor]
After a mistake or difficult moment, the mentor asks: "What is your mind telling you right now? Is it telling you that you DID something wrong, or that you ARE something wrong?" This simple check helps the child begin to distinguish guilt from shame in real time. The mentor corrects gently: "You did forget your chore. That is true. But that does not mean you are forgetful. It means you forgot today. What do you do now?"

### Practice 3: Correction Recovery [After Every Correction]
After receiving a correction, the child practices the sequence: listen, acknowledge, fix, move on. The "move on" is critical — the child does not carry the correction for hours. Once the repair is made, the interaction is finished. The mentor reinforces this: "You fixed it. We are done. Go play." No lingering guilt. No extended processing.

### Practice 4: Trying Again After Failure [As Opportunities Arise]
When the child fails at a task — a tower falls, a drawing goes wrong, a recipe does not work — the mentor does not rescue and does not dismiss. They say: "What happened? What would you do differently? Do you want to try again?" The decision to try again is the child's. The mentor creates the space but does not force it. Over time, the child chooses to try again more readily and with less emotional resistance.

### Practice 5: Stories of Failure and Recovery [Weekly]
At least once a week, read aloud a story where someone fails and recovers. This is not the same as a story where someone succeeds. The story must include the failure — the mistake, the consequence, the bad feeling — and then the recovery. Discuss: What did they do wrong? How did they feel? What did they do next? What would have happened if they had given up?

### Practice 6: Practicing Forgiveness [As Opportunities Arise]
When another child or sibling wrongs your student and tries to make repair, the mentor guides the forgiveness process: "She said she is sorry and she is trying to fix it. Can you accept that?" The child learns that accepting an apology is a real thing — not words you say automatically, but a decision to let the person move forward. The mentor also models this: when the child apologizes sincerely, the mentor accepts it cleanly and does not revisit it.

mentor_guide.formation_environment:
### The Guilt/Shame Distinction — Your Most Important Tool

The conceptual core of IC1.4 is the distinction between guilt and shame. This is not therapeutic vocabulary imported for decoration. It is a functional distinction with immediate practical consequences.

**Guilt** says: "I did something wrong." It is specific, bounded, and action-oriented. It motivates repair. A child experiencing guilt thinks: "I broke that. I need to fix it."

**Shame** says: "I am wrong." It is global, identity-level, and paralyzing. It motivates hiding, lying, or despair. A child experiencing shame thinks: "I broke that. I always break things. Something is wrong with me."

**Your correction determines which one the child experiences.** This is not entirely true — temperament, history, and neurology all play a role — but your response is the single largest variable you control.

Correction that produces guilt (useful):
- "You left your shoes in the hallway. Go put them away."
- "You hit your brother. We do not hit. Go tell him you are sorry."
- "You forgot to feed the dog. Go do it now."

Correction that produces shame (destructive):
- "What is wrong with you? Why can you never remember?"
- "I cannot believe you did that. What kind of person does that?"
- "You always do this. You never learn."

The first set addresses behavior. The second set addresses identity. The first says "this action was wrong." The second says "you are wrong." The child internalizes whichever message they receive most often.

### Your Emotional Regulation Is the Foundation

You cannot teach a child to process failure calmly if you process their failure with emotional volatility. A parent who screams when the child spills milk is teaching the child that mistakes provoke unpredictable adult reactions — which teaches the child to hide mistakes rather than face them.

This does not mean you feel nothing when your child makes a mistake. It means your response is governed by the principle, not by the feeling. You may be frustrated. You may be exhausted. You may be dealing with the same forgotten chore for the fifteenth time this month. Your correction is still calm, specific, and proportionate.

**The formula:**
1. Name the behavior: "You left the gate open."
2. Name the consequence or expectation: "The dog could get out. We always close the gate."
3. Direct the repair: "Go close it now."
4. Follow through: verify it is done.
5. It is finished. No lecture. No recounting of previous failures. No "we talked about this." Done.

**What if you fail at this?** You will. You will lose your temper. You will say something sharper than you meant. You will do the very thing this packet tells you not to do. When that happens, model IC1.4: own it, apologize specifically, and change your behavior. "I should not have yelled. That was not about you — I was frustrated about something else. I am sorry." The child learns more from watching you recover from your own failure than from any other practice in this packet.

### The Environment That Makes Truth-Telling Possible

A child will only tell the truth about their mistakes if the truth is less dangerous than the lie. If admitting a mistake produces a disproportionate reaction — rage, extended punishment, shaming — the child learns that the rational move is concealment. They will lie. Not because they are dishonest by nature, but because you have made honesty the more costly option.

**The principle:** The consequence for the mistake should always be less severe than the consequence for the cover-up. A child who breaks a vase and tells you should face the natural consequence (cleaning up, losing the privilege associated with the activity that broke it). A child who breaks a vase and lies about it faces a larger consequence — not for the vase, but for the dishonesty. The message: mistakes are manageable; hiding them makes everything worse.

**The practice:** When a child comes to you with a confession — "I broke this" or "I forgot to do that" — your first response matters enormously. Before correction, before consequence, acknowledge the courage: "Thank you for telling me the truth. That took courage." Then proceed with correction. This does not eliminate the consequence. It ensures the child learns that honesty is honored even when the news is bad.

### No Permanent Labels

Children are narrative creatures. They build stories about themselves from the words they hear most often. A child who is told "you are so careless" will build that into their identity — and will then behave carelessly, because that is who they are. A child who is told "you were careless with that — be more careful next time" receives information about behavior without receiving an identity.

**Never:**
- "You are lazy." (Identity label)
- "You are irresponsible." (Identity label)
- "You are a liar." (Identity label)
- "You always..." or "You never..." (Identity pattern — globalizing a single incident)

**Instead:**
- "You did not finish that task. Go complete it." (Behavior)
- "You forgot your responsibility. What do you need to do?" (Behavior)
- "You did not tell the truth about that. We tell the truth in this family." (Behavior + standard)
- "You made that mistake today. What will you do differently tomorrow?" (Behavior + forward action)

The shift is small in words and enormous in effect. Behavioral correction gives the child something to change. Identity labeling gives the child something to become.
mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Facing Mistakes Directly

**Developing well:** The child admits mistakes without extensive prompting. When something goes wrong, they tell you what happened rather than waiting for you to discover it. They may still be upset, but they face the situation rather than fleeing from it. On some occasions, they volunteer the information before being asked.

**Developing slowly:** The child habitually lies about mistakes, blames others, or hides evidence. Admission only comes after extensive questioning or after being caught. The first instinct is always concealment.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the consequence for honesty disproportionate? (If the child faces the same punishment whether they confess or are caught, there is no incentive for confession.) Is the child afraid of your reaction? (Observe their body language when they approach you with bad news — if they are bracing for impact, your reaction history has taught them to expect pain.) Is another adult or older sibling shaming the child for mistakes? (The formation environment includes everyone who interacts with the child regularly.)

### 2. Initiating Repair

**Developing well:** The child moves toward repair after a mistake — cleaning up a spill, apologizing after an unkind word, redoing a task that was done poorly. Initially this requires prompting ("What do we do?"), but over time the child begins to self-initiate. The repair may not be perfect — the towel leaves the table damp, the apology is mumbled — but the instinct to fix rather than flee is present.

**Developing slowly:** The child freezes after mistakes, waiting for an adult to resolve the situation. Or the child melts down emotionally in a way that makes the mistake about their feelings rather than about the repair needed. Or the child deflects: "It was not my fault" or "She made me do it."

**Diagnostic questions:** Has repair been modeled? (Does the child see adults in the household initiate repair after their own mistakes?) Is the repair expected age-appropriate? (A 4-year-old cannot repair complex social damage. They can wipe a table.) Is the child given the opportunity to repair, or does the adult handle it for them? (Over-functioning adults produce under-functioning children.)

### 3. Receiving Correction Calmly

**Developing well:** The child receives correction without meltdown on most occasions. They may not like it — their face may show displeasure, they may sigh or look away — but they comply without screaming, hitting, throwing things, or extended crying that outlasts the severity of the correction. They can return to normal function within minutes.

**Developing slowly:** Correction produces reliable emotional escalation — screaming, crying for extended periods, running away, shutting down completely, or aggressive behavior. The child's response to correction is consistently disproportionate to the correction itself.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the correction itself disproportionate? (A child who is screamed at will scream back or shut down — the problem is the correction, not the child.) Is the child getting enough sleep, food, and physical activity? (A child whose basic regulation systems are depleted will over-react to everything.) Is there a pattern to which corrections produce the biggest reactions? (Some children are more sensitive about certain types of failure — this is diagnostic information, not a problem to be fixed by ignoring it.) Is correction being delivered at the wrong time? (Correcting a child who is already flooded — hungry, tired, mid-tantrum — produces worse outcomes. Wait for regulation, then correct.)

### 4. Trying Again After Failure

**Developing well:** The child returns to activities and tasks after failure. They may need a break — walking away, calming down, doing something else for a while — but they come back. The time between failure and retry is decreasing over months. The child shows reduced emotional resistance to re-engaging with things that went wrong.

**Developing slowly:** The child avoids anything they have failed at. They refuse to return to a game they lost, a puzzle they could not complete, a physical skill they struggled with. The pattern is one of widening avoidance — the list of things the child "will not do" grows over time as each failure adds another item to the avoidance list.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the child being pushed to retry before they have recovered emotionally? (Forced retry when a child is still flooded teaches them that failure is followed by more pressure, not by space.) Is the difficulty level appropriate? (A child who repeatedly fails at a task far beyond their developmental level is not building resilience — they are being set up for repeated failure without learning.) Has the child ever experienced the satisfaction of succeeding after failure? (This experience is what makes retry feel worthwhile. If the child has never experienced it, manufactured success opportunities at the right difficulty level may be needed.)

### 5. Guilt/Shame Differentiation

**Developing well:** The child can distinguish, with prompting, between "I did something wrong" and "I am something wrong." When asked "Is your mind telling you that you did something bad, or that you ARE something bad?" the child can identify which one is happening. They may begin to self-correct: "I keep thinking I am stupid, but I am not stupid — I just got that wrong."

**Developing slowly:** The child globalizes every failure — one mistake becomes evidence of permanent inadequacy. "I can not do anything right." "I am the worst." "Nobody likes me because I always mess up." The child is unable to separate the incident from the identity.

**Diagnostic questions:** Who in the child's world uses identity-level language? (Children absorb the framing they hear most often. If anyone — parent, grandparent, teacher, older sibling — uses "you always" or "you are" language about failures, the child will internalize it.) Does the child show signs of anxiety or perfectionism? (Some children arrive at shame-proneness through temperament. These children need more explicit and more frequent guilt/shame coaching, not less.) Is the child hearing positive identity language as well? (A child who only hears identity language in the context of failure will understandably conclude that their identity is defined by failure.)

### 6. Accepting Consequences

**Developing well:** The child accepts natural consequences of mistakes without extended protest. They may not be happy — protest is allowed — but the protest does not outlast the consequence. If they lost a privilege because of a behavior, they accept it and move on. They do not relitigate.

**Developing slowly:** Every consequence produces extended negotiation, emotional escalation, or sustained grievance. The child treats every consequence as unjust. They cannot distinguish between a consequence and a punishment, or between proportionate and disproportionate responses.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are the consequences actually proportionate? (A child who protests a disproportionate consequence is showing good judgment, not poor character.) Are consequences consistent? (If the same behavior sometimes produces consequences and sometimes does not, the child will protest every time — because they know from experience that protesting sometimes works.) Are consequences natural or arbitrary? (Natural consequences — you left your bike in the rain, now it is rusty — teach more effectively than arbitrary ones — you left your bike in the rain, so no dessert.)

### 7. Emotional Recovery Time

**Developing well:** After a mistake and its correction, the child returns to baseline emotional function within a reasonable timeframe for their age. A 5-year-old who recovers in 10-15 minutes is doing well. An 8-year-old who recovers in 5-10 minutes is doing well. The key indicator is that they re-engage with normal activity rather than remaining stuck in the emotional state of the failure.

**Developing slowly:** The child remains emotionally stuck after mistakes for extended periods — hours of sulking, refusing to participate in normal activity, repeatedly returning to the incident long after correction is complete. Or the child recovers instantly but shows no evidence of having processed anything — the mistake is simply ignored, and the same behavior recurs immediately.

### 8. Forgiving Others

**Developing well:** When another child apologizes sincerely, the student can accept the apology and move forward. They do not hold the mistake over the other person indefinitely. They show growing capacity to let go of grievances.

**Developing slowly:** The child holds grudges persistently. Apologies are not accepted or are accepted verbally but behaviorally rejected (continued cold treatment, repeated references to the offense). The child uses others' mistakes as leverage or as justification for their own poor behavior.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Shaming in Disguise
Many parents who would never say "you are bad" still communicate shame through tone, body language, and indirect language. Heavy sighs, eye rolls, "I cannot believe this," walking away in visible disgust — these communicate identity-level rejection as effectively as the words. Monitor not just what you say, but how you say it and what your body communicates. Your child reads your nonverbal signals more accurately than your words.

### Over-Processing
The opposite of shaming is not processing every mistake into an extended therapeutic conversation. A young child does not need a twenty-minute discussion about a spilled glass of water. They need: "Oops. Grab a towel." Some families over-correct against harshness by turning every minor incident into a feelings exploration. The child learns that mistakes are enormous events requiring extensive emotional labor — which is its own form of making mistakes feel bigger than they are.

### Rescuing
When a child makes a mistake, the instinct to fix it for them is strong — especially when the fix is faster and cleaner than what the child will produce. Resist this. A parent who cleans up every spill, apologizes on behalf of the child, and manages every consequence for them is producing a child who does not know how to manage failure independently. The mess the child cleans up imperfectly is more valuable than the mess you clean up perfectly.

### Punishing Honesty
If the consequence is the same whether the child confesses or is caught, or worse — if the child is punished more harshly for confessing because the confession draws attention to the mistake sooner — you are training the child to lie. The principle is firm: honesty is always acknowledged before correction, and the consequence for concealment is always greater than the consequence for the mistake itself.

### False Reassurance
"It is fine! Do not worry about it!" when the child has genuinely done something wrong teaches the child that mistakes have no real consequences — which is untrue and confusing. The opposite of shame is not minimization. The opposite of shame is honest, bounded accountability: "Yes, you did that. It was wrong. Here is what you do about it. Now it is over." The child needs to know that mistakes are real AND survivable, not that they are imaginary.

### Using Mistakes as Character Evidence
"This is just like the time you..." or "You did this same thing last week." Connecting current mistakes to past mistakes creates a narrative of persistent failure — an identity story. Each mistake should be addressed as its own event. If patterns are genuinely repeating, the mentor addresses the pattern structurally (is the routine clear? is the expectation age-appropriate? is there an underlying issue?) rather than narratively (presenting the child with a history of their failures).
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: A Time You Made a Mistake
**Strong:** Specific incident. Can describe what happened, how they felt, and what they did about it. Shows awareness that the mistake was fixable and that they took action. "I accidentally knocked over my sister's blocks. I felt bad. I helped her rebuild them."
**Weak:** Cannot recall a mistake (either cannot self-identify failures or is too ashamed to share one). Or recalls a mistake but with heavy shame language: "I always break things." Or deflects responsibility entirely: "It was not really my fault."

### Prompt 2: Doing Something Wrong vs. Being Wrong
**Strong:** Can distinguish, even in simple terms. "Doing something wrong means you made a mistake, like forgetting. Being wrong means you think you are bad." May need prompting to articulate but shows conceptual understanding when supported.
**Weak:** Cannot distinguish. Equates making a mistake with being a bad person. "If you do something wrong you ARE wrong."

### Prompt 3: What to Do When You Break/Spill Something
**Strong:** Describes repair steps. "Clean it up. Tell someone. See if you can fix it." The sequence shows action orientation — the instinct is to fix, not to hide or to feel bad.
**Weak:** "Tell mom." (Reliance on adult to manage rather than self-initiation.) Or: "Say sorry." (Only apologize, without repair action.) Or: "I do not know." (No internalized repair sequence.)

### Prompt 4: Trying Again After Failure
**Strong:** Can describe a specific failure and a specific retry. Shows awareness of what changed between the first attempt and the second. "My tower kept falling down. I tried making the bottom bigger and it worked."
**Weak:** Cannot recall trying again — avoidance pattern. Or recalls retry but with heavy emotional distress at the memory, suggesting the failure is still unprocessed.

### Prompt 5: How Correction Feels
**Strong:** Honest about the discomfort: "I do not like it. My face gets hot. But then I fix it and it is over." Shows awareness that correction is temporary and leads to resolution.
**Weak:** "It is fine." (Performative dismissal — the child is not engaging honestly.) Or: "I hate it. It means I am bad." (Shame response — correction is experienced as identity attack.)

### Prompt 6: Forgiving Someone
**Strong:** Can recall a specific instance of forgiving or being forgiven. Shows understanding that forgiveness means letting the person move forward after repair. "My friend said sorry and I said okay and we played again."
**Weak:** Cannot recall forgiving anyone, or describes holding grudges as normal. "I do not forgive people because they should not have done it." (Rigid, punitive frame.)
